title: The Forgotten God
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: Israel's Claim and God's Charge
subpoints: Ian Murray's biography of Spurgeon was called 'The Forgotten Spurgeon'; Hosea 8 could be called 'The Forgotten God'
Israel claims in verse 2, 'My God, we know you,' yet God charges them in verse 14: 'Israel has forgotten his Maker'
Four applications on forgetting God: not living by his Word; living autonomously; making worship self-centered; inviting divine judgment

outline.title: 1. We Forget God When We Don't Live According to His Word
subpoints: Verse 1: Israel has transgressed God's covenant; verse 14: Israel has forgotten its Maker
Back in Hosea 6:7 and Exodus 24, Israel promised obedience to God but quickly abandoned it to become a law to themselves
God and his Word are so united that you cannot remember God without following his Word — the Son is himself called 'the Word'
Israel's defense — 'My God, we know you' — is refuted by the evidence: prostitution, false worship, rejecting the good (v.3)

outline.title: 2. We Forget God When We Live Autonomously
subpoints: To live autonomously is to be a law to yourself, captain of your own soul — Israel set up kings without consulting God and made idols from silver and gold
Verse 5-6: God asks, 'How long until they attain to innocence?' — Israel was so entrenched in forgetfulness that change seemed impossible, yet God's mercy always makes repentance possible
Key diagnostic questions: Is the way I'm living distinctively Christian? How is God's glory shaping my marriage, parenting, finances, time, work, worship, relationships?
How you spend your time and money is the ultimate display of what is important to you — not what you say is important

outline.title: 3. We Forget God When Worship Is About Ourselves
subpoints: Verse 13: 'For the sacrifices of my offerings they sacrifice flesh and eat. The Lord does not accept them' — Israel only offered sacrifices they could eat; the point was the meal, not God
We easily look to church for what we can get out of it rather than to worship God and bless others; convenience has become a measure of our dedication
Verse 11: 'Ephraim has made many altars for sin; they have become for him altars of sinning' — Israel planned to sin and so they sinned; we must destroy our altars for sinning
If you build provisions for sin you will sin: what altars to sport, work, entertainment, or finances have you built that crowd out the worship of God?

outline.title: 4. Divine Judgment Falls on Those Who Live as if They Don't Know God
subpoints: Verse 7: 'They sow the wind and reap the whirlwind' — the Assyrian captivity was the harvest of autonomous rebellion
We struggle with God's severe judgment because we think our sins are small indiscretions and that God should not be so bothered — but that reveals we think ourselves too big and God too small
We are more like the Pharisee in Luke 18:9-14 than we like to admit — our own righteousness before God is like filthy rags (Isaiah 64:6)
The prayer God always hears: 'God, be merciful to me, a sinner' — we are justified only because of our union with Christ and his righteousness

outline.title: Conclusion: Look to Christ, Look to the Life to Come
subpoints: Even Hosea 8's severe judgment begins with mercy: 'Set the trumpet to your mouth' — God warns so his people can repent and be restored
Paul's testimony in 2 Corinthians 1:12: 'We conduct ourselves in the world in simplicity and Godly sincerity, not with fleshly wisdom but by the grace of God'
The blessings are in the ages to come in Christ Jesus; look to that city whose foundation is God, to the new Jerusalem, to the coming of the Lord who makes all things new
Every day lived in the light of the glory to come will be marked by remembrance of God

summary:
Hosea 8 is what Pastor Tito Lyro calls 'The Forgotten God.' Although Israel insists in verse 2, 'My God, we know you,' God charges them plainly in verse 14: 'Israel has forgotten his Maker.' The sermon draws out four ways we forget God: by failing to live according to his Word, by living autonomously as a law to ourselves, by making worship self-serving, and by inviting the divine judgment that follows a life lived as if God does not exist.

The sermon presses hard on practical application: how God's glory and the enjoyment of him should shape marriage, parenting, finances, time, work, worship, and relationships. The uncomfortable reality, says Pastor Lyro, is that how we spend our time and money reveals what is actually important to us — not our words. He challenges listeners to demolish 'altars for sinning' — planned provisions for neglecting worship — that crowd out devotion to God.

The sermon closes with a call to honest self-examination using the parable of the Pharisee and the tax collector. Like the Pharisee, we tend to compare ourselves favorably with others rather than measuring against the perfect law of God. We are only worthy before God because of our union with Christ. The chapter's opening trumpet call is itself an act of mercy — God warns so his people can repent. Looking to Christ and to the life to come is the antidote to forgetting God in the everyday.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Forgotten God

I want to thank Pastor Leeman and Isaiah Couch for preaching last week in my absence.

Hosea chapter 8, starting at verse 1:

*Set the trumpet to your mouth. He shall come like an eagle against the house of the Lord because they have transgressed my covenant and rebelled against my law. Israel cried to me, 'My God, we know you.' Israel has rejected the good. The enemy will pursue him. They set up kings but not by me. They make princes but I do not acknowledge them. From their silver and gold they made idols for themselves, that they might be cut off. Your calf is rejected, O Samaria. My anger is aroused against them. How long until they attain to innocence? For from Israel is even this: a workman made it, and it is not God; but the calf of Samaria shall be broken to pieces. They sow the wind and reap the whirlwind. The stalk has no bud; it shall never produce meal. If it should produce, aliens would swallow it up. Israel is swallowed up; now they are among the Gentiles like a vessel in which is no pleasure. For they have gone up to Assyria, like a wild donkey alone by itself. Ephraim has hired lovers. Yes, though they have hired among the nations, now I will gather them, and they shall sorrow a little because of the burden of the king and princes. Because Ephraim has made many altars for sin, they have become for him altars for sinning. I have written for him the great things of my law, but they were considered a strange thing. For the sacrifices of my offerings they sacrifice flesh and eat it. The Lord does not accept them. He will remember their iniquity and punish their sins. They shall return to Egypt. For Israel has forgotten his Maker and has built temples. Judah also has multiplied fortified cities; but I will send a fire upon his cities, and it shall devour his palaces.*

## Introduction: Four Applications on Forgetting God

Years ago, Ian Murray — who started the Banner of Truth Trust in England — wrote a biography of the great English preacher Charles Haddon Spurgeon. He called it *The Forgotten Spurgeon*, wanting to recover aspects of Spurgeon's life and theology that other biographers had missed. We could call Hosea 8 *The Forgotten God*. Israel has forgotten God. God charges them with that in verse 14. They claim in verse 2 that God is still their God, but God says: no, you have forgotten me.

I fear that we professing believers in the Lord Jesus Christ often forget God and his Word in everyday life, in the same way that Israel portrayed in Hosea 8 has forgotten God. They profess faith in Christ, but everything about their lives tells us that they do not remember God.

As we work through this chapter, I want you to notice four applications, and they all have to do with the idea of forgetting God.

First: we forget God when we do not live according to his Word — verses 1-3 and verse 14.

Second: we forget God when we live autonomously — when we are a law to ourselves.

Third: we forget God when worship is about ourselves — verses 11-13.

Fourth: divine judgment comes upon those who live as if they do not know God — verses 7-10.

## 1. We Forget God When We Don't Live According to His Word

The charge God brings against Israel through Hosea is that Israel has forgotten its Maker: 'For Israel has forgotten his Maker and has built temples.' They are going about life as if there is nothing wrong, as if they are the greatest people ever — yet they have forgotten their Maker.

Why is God saying that Israel has forgotten him? Because Israel has disregarded God's Word in the way they live. In verse 1, God says: 'Israel has transgressed my covenant.' We saw back in chapter 6, verse 7 that Israel had broken the Mosaic covenant by being a law to themselves. Back in Exodus 24, Israel stood before God and said, 'We will do whatever you tell us.' That did not last very long.

This is very important for all of us to realize: God and his Word are so united that you cannot remember God without following his Word. You can say you believe in God until you are blue in the face. But if you are not following what he says, the God you believe in is not God Almighty, Creator of heaven and earth. The union between God and his Word is so powerful that God the Son in the Gospel of John calls himself the Word.

And yet Israel pleads, 'My God, we know you.' They claim that how they are living is exactly what God wanted them to do. They are not saying they are serving another God. They are saying, 'Lord, why are you upset with us? The way we are living is just what you want.' Yet any reading of Hosea shows that is not the case: they are prostituting themselves, worshiping false gods, and yet say, 'Lord, we are doing all this unto you.'

All the evidence is that Israel has rejected the good — verse 3: 'Israel has rejected the good; the enemy will pursue him.' Notice God does not say that Israel simply rejected what is good; he says Israel rejected *the good*. That article is important. The good here is God's presence in their lives through his covenant. They forsook the good — God's presence — by abandoning his Word. Rebellion is the opposite of the good, however much it may feel like freedom at first.

Notice also the mercy of the Lord in the midst of judgment. The very first words of chapter 8 are: 'Set the trumpet to your mouth.' Hosea, blow the ram's horn — let the people know what is coming so they can repent and be restored. That was the message of Jesus himself. His first recorded sermon: 'The time is fulfilled, the kingdom of God is at hand — repent and believe in the gospel.' It is the mercy of the Lord to warn his people of impending judgment so that they can repent.

## 2. We Forget God When We Live Autonomously

To live autonomously is to be a law to yourself — to live as if nothing outside of you has any claim on your life. Israel is doing this. They crowned their own kings without consulting God. They made idols to worship — idols they justified as worship of Yahweh, as if they could worship God however they chose without any regard to what his Word says about worship.

In verse 5, God asks how long until they attain to innocence. A better translation: 'How long will they be incapable of innocence?' Israel is so entrenched in forgetfulness that God wonders — using language we understand — whether they are even capable of ever remembering him. In God's great mercy the answer is always: yes, it is never too late. As long as there is breath in our lungs, we can turn to the Lord in repentance and faith.

We read passages like this and we go, 'How can they be so bad?' And then there we are — his brothers and sisters — often doing exactly what Israel is doing in Hosea 8. We say that God is our God and that we know him, but then we live as if he did not exist. We do not pray about life. We do not consider God in making decisions. We do not ask ourselves: how is the way I am living my life distinctively Christian?

If the reason I exist, the reason God made me, is to glorify God and enjoy him now and forever — how is that shaping my life? How is God's glory shaping my marriage — and I mean you, not your spouse? How is it shaping the way you raise your kids? How is it shaping your finances? The reality of our church is that a great number of our members do not tithe. The church is not in financial trouble, but when you look at how many people come and how much money comes in, it is obvious that many do not tithe — and that is a spiritual matter.

How is God's glory shaping the way you spend your time? It seems like church-related activities are the first casualty of an overcommitted schedule. This may make people uncomfortable, but I believe it completely: you can say whatever you want about what is important to you, but how you spend your time and money is the ultimate display of what is important. The measurables of your life show what matters.

## 3. We Forget God When Worship Is About Ourselves

We see this in verses 11-13. Verse 13: 'For the sacrifices of my offerings they sacrifice flesh and eat it. The Lord does not accept them.' God is saying: they only offer the sacrifices they can eat. While eating the sacrifice was prescribed in Scripture as a means to understand forgiveness and God's goodness, Israel had turned it into an end in itself. The sacrifices were not about God — they were about themselves.

How often do we do the same? We look to church for what we can get out of it — how we can benefit — not to worship God and bless others. We are very comfortable with the expression that somebody is 'shopping for a church' like shopping for a dress or a car. Brothers and sisters, church is not to be shopped for. Church is where we worship God Almighty, Creator of heaven and earth, and our Savior the Lord Jesus Christ. And if church gets in the way of other things, or is just inconvenient, we give it up.

Do not use convenience as a measure of your dedication to the worship of the Lord and the fellowship of the saints. It was not convenient for our Savior to be crucified — yet he did it. The inconvenience of the cross did not keep him from being crucified for you, and rising from the dead for you.

Notice verse 11: 'Because Ephraim has made many altars for sin, they have become for him altars for sinning.' Ephraim decided to set up altars so that they could sin — and because they planned to sin, they sinned. We often wonder why our spiritual lives are shriveling when we neglect the worship of God and the fellowship of the saints. It is because we planned to neglect them.

Brothers and sisters — especially you men — the decision to come to church is a decision you make once. Not every week. You make it once, and that is it: on the Lord's day we will be in the Lord's house. What altars for sinning have you built in your heart? Sports — because your kid needs to practice? Work on Sundays — for financial prosperity? Entertainment? Destroy them, by the grace of God, destroy them.

## 4. Divine Judgment Falls on Those Who Live as If They Don't Know God

We see this in verses 7-10. Verse 7: 'They sow the wind and reap the whirlwind.' The whirlwind of Assyrian captivity was sown in the wind of autonomous rebellion. We struggle with the idea of God judging Israel so severely. We also struggle with the idea of hell. And we struggle because at the bottom of our hearts we think Israel — and we ourselves — do not deserve such severe judgment. We think we are worthy of more, and that God is less of a being than he is.

We tend to think we deserve God's blessings, that he owes us. We think our sins are little indiscretions — if they are sins at all. We do not have a real need for Jesus, after all, because only the sick or the lost need a Savior, and we are pretty good. We are not perfect, but — and notice the word 'but' — what does that 'but' mean? It means we actually think we are pretty close to perfect.

Brothers and sisters, we are much more like Israel than we would like to admit. We are more like the Pharisee in Luke 18:9-14 than like the tax collector. The Lord Jesus said two men went up to the temple to pray — a Pharisee and a tax collector. The Pharisee stood and prayed: 'God, I thank you that I am not like other men — extortioners, unjust, adulterers, or even like this tax collector. I fast twice a week; I give tithes of all that I possess.'

We can always find someone who is worse than us. But that is not the measure. The measure is the perfect law of God.

The tax collector, standing far off, would not so much as raise his eyes to heaven, but beat his breast saying: 'God, be merciful to me, a sinner.' Jesus says: this man went down to his house justified rather than the other. For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted.

We parade our own righteousness before God as reasons why he should be thankful we believe in him — rather than clinging to Jesus for dear life, knowing that the very best we can offer to God is like filthy rags, according to Isaiah. When we bring our best righteousness to God, all that is inside is the dirtiest clothes imaginable. We do this because we see ourselves as too big and God as too small.

We are only worthy before God because of our union with Christ. As Paul says in Ephesians 2:4-7 — one of the most glorious 'but God' passages in the Bible: 'But God, who is rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us, even when we were dead in trespasses, made us alive together with Christ... that in the ages to come he might show the exceeding riches of his grace in his kindness toward us in Christ Jesus.'

## Conclusion: Look to Christ, Look to the Life to Come

The blessings Paul describes are in the ages to come in Christ Jesus — look to that. Look to the city whose foundation is God. Look to the new Jerusalem built by the Lord. Look to the coming of the Lord Jesus Christ in which he is going to make all things new.

And as we do that, every day — Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, and every other day — will be lived in the light of the glory of the Son, as the mind of God, as we live always mindful of him.

Father, we thank you that you are our God through Jesus Christ. We pray that we live according to your word, that we not forget you, that we not forget Jesus and what he has done for us. We are asking in his name. Amen.
